By unlocking the "dark" informational content of nuclear spin ensembles, our work establishes a generalizable paradigm for goal-driven quantum state engineering in magnetic resonance and beyond.

AI.tldr: A differentiable physical framework uses automatic differentiation to design non-intuitive quantum states for MRS, enabling robust separation of Glutamate and Glutamine at 3T.
AI.motivation: Traditional MRS pulse sequence design is limited by human intuition and focuses on simple quantum states, missing the vast potential of complex spin superpositions in the exponentially scaling operator space. This restricts spectral resolution and sensitivity, particularly for challenging metabolite separations like Glutamate and Glutamine.
AI.method: Developed a spectrum-driven, end-to-end differentiable physical framework that integrates physical laws with automatic differentiation algorithms. This approach directly navigates high-dimensional spin dynamics space, bypassing the intractable inverse problem of state preparation to discover non-intuitive, complex mixed states.
AI.result: Achieved robust separation of Glutamate and Glutamine in the human brain at 3T, demonstrating spectral fidelity superior to conventional methods. This addresses a longstanding neuroimaging challenge.
AI.conclusion: The framework unlocks the "dark" informational content of nuclear spin ensembles and establishes a generalizable paradigm for goal-driven quantum state engineering in magnetic resonance and beyond, transcending heuristic limitations of traditional pulse sequence design.

title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
summary: We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.

AI.tldr: A scheme for distributing pairwise entanglement in quantum networks using environmental ancilla qubits and tunable Hamiltonians, analyzed through collision and repeated interaction models.
AI.motivation: To develop a systematic and physically implementable method for controllable entanglement distribution in quantum networks, addressing the need for selective entanglement generation between different qubit pairs including non-neighboring nodes.
AI.method: Uses environmental ancilla qubits interacting with network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians within a collision model framework. Two scenarios: traditional collision model (ancilla reset after each interaction) and repeated interaction model (ancilla state carried over). System-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps, and varying interaction patterns enables selective entanglement generation.
AI.result: Demonstrates generation of maximally entangled Bell pairs in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, even when the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Shows selective entanglement generation between different qubit pairs including non-neighboring nodes.
AI.conclusion: Provides a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution with potential applications in quantum communication, metrology, and modular quantum computing.

title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
summary: Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required. In this work, we present a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks. It enables the receiver to verify compliance with agreed-upon policies. At the same time, the protocol preserves the operator's confidentiality by ensuring that no sensitive information about the network topology is revealed to users. We provide a formal model and a provably secure generic construction of the protocol, along with a concrete instantiation. For long-distance communication involving 100 nodes, the protocol has a computational cost of 1-2.5s depending on the machine, and a communication overhead of less than 70kB - demonstrating the efficiency of our approach.

AI.tldr: A path validation protocol for QKD networks that allows receivers to verify policy compliance while preserving network topology confidentiality, with efficient performance for large-scale deployments.
AI.motivation: In QKD networks, trust must extend beyond repeater nodes to network operators who must ensure compliance with contractual obligations like certified device usage and disjoint transmission paths, requiring verifiable policy compliance without revealing sensitive network topology information.
AI.method: Developed a formal model and provably secure generic construction of a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks, with a concrete instantiation that preserves operator confidentiality while enabling receiver verification.
AI.result: The protocol achieves efficient performance: computational cost of 1-2.5 seconds for 100-node long-distance communication (depending on machine), with communication overhead less than 70kB, demonstrating practical feasibility for large-scale deployments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed path validation protocol successfully addresses the dual requirements of QKD networks: enabling receivers to verify policy compliance while preserving operator confidentiality regarding network topology, with efficient performance suitable for real-world deployment.

title: Curvature-induced bound states in quantum wires
comment: 21 pages, 5 figures, submitted to Phys. Rev. Research
summary: A classical particle under spatial constraints is strictly confined to live on a specific space manifold or path, but this assumption is incompatible with the zero-point fluctuations of a quantum particle. One way to describe quantum mechanics under constraints is the confinement potential approach (CPA). For a non-relativistic particle, the CPA maps the problem onto the solution of a Schrödinger-type equation in an isometrically embedded Riemannian submanifold of Euclidean space while the motion along orthogonal directions are decoupled and spatially confined. This approach respects quantum uncertainty, and one of its key results is the appearance of geometry- and metric-induced potentials that affect the stationary states and the dynamics of the particle. For particles constrained to different spaces, such as structures hosting sharp bents, vertices, wedges, conical apices, tips, or self-intersections, a formalism beyond the CPA is needed. Here, a step towards a CPA extension for irregular spaces is presented. After classifying the possible geometric irregularities concerning the CPA formalism, the presentation is focused on a sharply bent quantum wire modeled as an embedded curve with singular (but absolute integrable) curvature. For a subclass fulfilling the additional requirement that the geometric potential is a distribution of first order, a solution scheme for the confined Schrödinger equation is presented based on singular Sturm-Liouville theory and operator theoretic methods. The analytical considerations and numerical simulations evidence the existence of curvature-induced bound states with non-differentiable wave functions localized around the singular point, with an extension well beyond the singularity. Furthermore, a multitude of scattering states appear that may affect the transport and optical properties of the system.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the confinement potential approach (CPA) for quantum particles in irregular spaces with geometric singularities, focusing on sharply bent quantum wires with singular curvature, revealing curvature-induced bound states and scattering states.
AI.motivation: Classical spatial constraints are incompatible with quantum zero-point fluctuations. While CPA works for regular manifolds, a formalism beyond CPA is needed for irregular spaces with geometric singularities like sharp bends, vertices, wedges, conical apices, tips, or self-intersections.
AI.method: Classifies geometric irregularities concerning CPA formalism, focuses on sharply bent quantum wires modeled as embedded curves with singular (but absolutely integrable) curvature. For a subclass where geometric potential is a first-order distribution, develops solution scheme using singular Sturm-Liouville theory and operator theoretic methods, complemented by numerical simulations.
AI.result: Existence of curvature-induced bound states with non-differentiable wave functions localized around singular points, extending well beyond the singularity. Also reveals multitude of scattering states that may affect transport and optical properties of the system.
AI.conclusion: Presents a step toward extending CPA for irregular spaces with geometric singularities, demonstrating that singular curvature in quantum wires leads to novel bound states and scattering phenomena with implications for quantum transport and optical properties.

title: Transversal non-Clifford gates on almost-good quantum LDPC and quantum locally testable codes
summary: We exhibit nontrivial transversal logical multi-controlled-$Z$ gates on $[\![N,Θ(N),\tildeΘ(N)]\!]$ quantum low-density parity-check codes and $[\![N,Θ(N),\tildeΘ(N)]\!]$ quantum locally testable codes with soundness $\tildeΘ(1)$, combining nearly optimal code parameters with fault-tolerant non-Clifford gates for the first time. Remarkably, our proofs are almost entirely algebraic-topological, showing that such presumably intricate logical gates naturally arise as a fundamental topological phenomenon. We develop a general framework for constructing a rich new family of homological invariant forms which we call ''cupcap gates'' that induce transversal logical multi-controlled-$Z$ and, building on insights from [Li et al., arXiv:2603.25831], covering space methods to certify their nontriviality. The claimed almost-good code results follow immediately as examples.

AI.tldr: The paper presents nontrivial transversal logical multi-controlled-Z gates on quantum LDPC and locally testable codes with near-optimal parameters, using algebraic-topological methods to construct "cupcap gates" that naturally arise as topological phenomena.
AI.motivation: To combine nearly optimal quantum code parameters with fault-tolerant non-Clifford gates for the first time, addressing the challenge of implementing logical multi-controlled-Z gates on high-performance quantum error-correcting codes.
AI.method: Develops an algebraic-topological framework constructing "cupcap gates" as homological invariant forms that induce transversal logical multi-controlled-Z gates, using covering space methods to certify their nontriviality.
AI.result: Achieves nontrivial transversal logical multi-controlled-Z gates on [N,Θ(N),Θ̃(N)] quantum LDPC codes and [N,Θ(N),Θ̃(N)] quantum locally testable codes with soundness Θ̃(1), demonstrating near-optimal code parameters with fault-tolerant non-Clifford gates.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that intricate logical gates naturally emerge as fundamental topological phenomena, providing a general framework for constructing fault-tolerant non-Clifford operations on high-performance quantum codes through algebraic-topological methods.

title: Phase-enhanced nonreciprocal photon-phonon conversion via coupled optomechanical cavities
summary: Nonreciprocity, characterized by direction-dependent signal propagation, is fundamental to technologies such as isolators, signal routing, and precision sensing. This letter theoretically demonstrates nonreciprocal phonon transport and the conversion between photon and acoustic phonon signals in coupled optomechanical cavities via phase-dependent driving. It is demonstrated that, in contrast to nonreciprocal phonon transport, which necessitates both dissipation and phase-induced violation of time reversal symmetry, the nonreciprocity in photon-phonon conversion can occur without violating time reversal symmetry. We demonstrate that such nonreciprocity arises due to the path-dependent asymmetry in photon-phonon conversion. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the nonreciprocity of photon-phonon conversion can be further enhanced, achieving isolation levels of up to 40 dB by suitably modifying the phase difference of the driving lasers.

AI.tldr: Nonreciprocal phonon transport and photon-phonon conversion in optomechanical cavities via phase-dependent driving, with different symmetry requirements for each process.
AI.motivation: Nonreciprocity is crucial for technologies like isolators, signal routing, and precision sensing. The paper aims to demonstrate nonreciprocal phonon transport and photon-phonon conversion in optomechanical systems.
AI.method: Theoretical demonstration using coupled optomechanical cavities with phase-dependent driving. Analysis of symmetry requirements for nonreciprocal phonon transport versus photon-phonon conversion.
AI.result: Nonreciprocal phonon transport requires both dissipation and phase-induced violation of time reversal symmetry, while photon-phonon conversion nonreciprocity can occur without violating time reversal symmetry due to path-dependent asymmetry. Isolation levels up to 40 dB achievable by modifying phase difference of driving lasers.
AI.conclusion: Different mechanisms govern nonreciprocity in phonon transport versus photon-phonon conversion in optomechanical systems, with photon-phonon conversion offering nonreciprocity without time reversal symmetry violation and achieving high isolation levels through phase control.

title: A Loop-Shaping Approach to Coherent Feedback Control in Cavity Optomechanical Cooling
summary: We present a loop-shaping approach to coherent feedback (CF) control. By formulating the coupling between a quantum system and its environment in terms of the noise power spectrum, our method enables direct manipulation of the effective dissipation coefficients through spectral shaping. A systematic design framework for CF controllers is also developed, in which transfer functions are shaped to realize desired spectral responses. Applying this framework to optomechanical sideband cooling, we demonstrate that suppression of the Stokes process and enhancement of the anti-Stokes process can be simultaneously achieved, enabling ground-state cooling even in the unresolved-sideband regime. This loop-shaping framework provides an intuitive and general foundation for the design of CF controllers and can be extended to a wide class of quantum systems in which interactions with environments are characterized by noise power spectra.

AI.tldr: Loop-shaping approach to coherent feedback control for quantum systems using noise power spectrum formulation to manipulate dissipation coefficients through spectral shaping.
AI.motivation: To develop an intuitive and systematic design framework for coherent feedback controllers in quantum systems by formulating system-environment coupling in terms of noise power spectra.
AI.method: Loop-shaping approach where transfer functions are shaped to realize desired spectral responses; coupling between quantum system and environment is formulated via noise power spectrum to enable direct manipulation of effective dissipation coefficients.
AI.result: Applied to optomechanical sideband cooling, the method enables simultaneous suppression of Stokes process and enhancement of anti-Stokes process, achieving ground-state cooling even in unresolved-sideband regime.
AI.conclusion: The loop-shaping framework provides an intuitive and general foundation for coherent feedback controller design, extendable to quantum systems where environment interactions are characterized by noise power spectra.

title: Universal critical timescales in slow non-Hermitian dynamics
summary: Non-Hermitian systems driven along slow parametric loops undergo non-adiabatic transitions whose outcome depends sensitively on the driving speed, yet no explicit formula has been available for the critical timescale $T_{\mathrm{cr}}$ at which these transitions develop. Using a $2\times 2$ Hamiltonian with circular parameter trajectories, we derive $T_{\mathrm{cr}} = \mathcal{G}\,\ln(1/|Δ|)$ in closed form for non-encircling loops, phase-shifted loops, offset loops, and loops encircling exceptional points, where $\mathcal{G}$ is a geometry-dependent growth factor and $Δ$ is the instability seed. This formula sharply separates the regime where the system remains in the averagely dominant eigenstate ($T< T_{\mathrm{cr}}$) from the superadiabatic regime where the instantaneous dominant eigenstate takes over ($T> T_{\mathrm{cr}}$), resolving the apparent tension between the previous literature. We identify two competing seeds: a geometric Stokes multiplier and the finite-precision floor. When the geometric seed vanishes, precision alone governs the transition, yielding $T_{\mathrm{cr}} \propto m\lnβ$, linear in the number of precision bits $m$. This provides a purely forward-evolution manifestation of precision-induced irreversibility (PIR)~\cite{PIR}, demonstrating that the fundamental limit identified through echo protocols also controls the outcome of slow non-Hermitian dynamics without requiring time reversal. For PT-symmetric energy spectra, $T_{\mathrm{cr}}$ additionally determines the onset of chirality: the dynamics is non-chiral for $T< T_{\mathrm{cr}}$ and chiral for $T> T_{\mathrm{cr}}$.

AI.tldr: Derivation of explicit formula for critical timescale T_cr in non-Hermitian systems undergoing non-adiabatic transitions, separating regimes of average vs instantaneous dominant eigenstate behavior.
AI.motivation: Non-Hermitian systems driven along slow parametric loops exhibit non-adiabatic transitions sensitive to driving speed, but no explicit formula existed for the critical timescale T_cr at which these transitions develop.
AI.method: Using a 2×2 Hamiltonian with circular parameter trajectories to derive T_cr = G ln(1/|Δ|) in closed form for various loop types (non-encircling, phase-shifted, offset, and loops encircling exceptional points), where G is geometry-dependent growth factor and Δ is instability seed.
AI.result: Formula sharply separates regimes: system remains in averagely dominant eigenstate for T < T_cr, while instantaneous dominant eigenstate takes over for T > T_cr (superadiabatic regime). Identifies two competing seeds: geometric Stokes multiplier and finite-precision floor. When geometric seed vanishes, precision alone governs transition, yielding T_cr ∝ m lnβ (linear in precision bits m), demonstrating precision-induced irreversibility. For PT-symmetric spectra, T_cr determines onset of chirality.
AI.conclusion: Provides explicit critical timescale formula resolving previous literature tension, reveals precision-induced irreversibility in forward evolution without time reversal, and establishes T_cr as fundamental limit controlling slow non-Hermitian dynamics outcomes.

title: Quantum-Inspired Geometric Classification with Correlation Group Structures and VQC Decision Modeling
summary: We propose a geometry-driven quantum-inspired classification framework that integrates Correlation Group Structures (CGR), compact SWAP-test-based overlap estimation, and selective variational quantum decision modelling. Rather than directly approximating class posteriors, the method adopts a geometry-first paradigm in which samples are evaluated relative to class medoids using overlap-derived Euclidean-like and angular similarity channels. CGR organizes features into anchor-centered correlation neighbourhoods, generating nonlinear, correlation-weighted representations that enhance robustness in heterogeneous tabular spaces. These geometric signals are fused through a non-probabilistic margin-based fusion score, serving as a lightweight and data-efficient primary classifier for small-to-moderate datasets. On Heart Disease, Breast Cancer, and Wine Quality datasets, the fusion-score classifier achieves 0.8478, 0.8881, and 0.9556 test accuracy respectively, with macro-F1 scores of 0.8463, 0.8703, and 0.9522, demonstrating competitive and stable performance relative to classical baselines. For large-scale and highly imbalanced regimes, we construct compact Delta-distance contrastive features and train a variational quantum classifier (VQC) as a nonlinear refinement layer. On the Credit Card Fraud dataset (0.17% prevalence), the Delta + VQC pipeline achieves approximately 0.85 minority recall at an alert rate of approximately 1.31%, with ROC-AUC 0.9249 and PR-AUC 0.3251 under full-dataset evaluation. These results highlight the importance of operating-point-aware assessment in rare-event detection and demonstrate that the proposed hybrid geometric-variational framework provides interpretable, scalable, and regime-adaptive classification across heterogeneous data settings.

AI.tldr: A hybrid quantum-inspired classification framework combining geometric correlation structures with variational quantum refinement for heterogeneous tabular data, achieving competitive performance across standard datasets and effective rare-event detection.
AI.motivation: To develop a classification framework that addresses heterogeneous tabular data challenges by integrating geometric principles with quantum-inspired methods, providing interpretable and regime-adaptive solutions across different data scales and imbalance scenarios.
AI.method: 1) Geometry-driven approach using Correlation Group Structures (CGR) to organize features into anchor-centered correlation neighborhoods; 2) Compact SWAP-test-based overlap estimation for similarity measurement; 3) Non-probabilistic margin-based fusion score as primary classifier; 4) For large/imbalanced data: Delta-distance contrastive features + variational quantum classifier refinement layer.
AI.result: On standard datasets: Heart Disease (0.8478 accuracy, 0.8463 F1), Breast Cancer (0.8881 accuracy, 0.8703 F1), Wine Quality (0.9556 accuracy, 0.9522 F1). On Credit Card Fraud (0.17% prevalence): ~0.85 minority recall at ~1.31% alert rate, ROC-AUC 0.9249, PR-AUC 0.3251.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid geometric-variational framework provides interpretable, scalable, and regime-adaptive classification across heterogeneous data settings, demonstrating the importance of operating-point-aware assessment in rare-event detection and competitive performance relative to classical baselines.

title: Towards Chemically Accurate and Scalable Quantum Simulations on IQM Quantum Hardware: A Quantum-HPC Hybrid Approach
summary: We present a large-scale experimental study of quantum-computing-based molecular simulation carried out on IQM's Sirius 24-qubit superconducting processor, utilizing up to 16 operational qubits. The work employs Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) together with the Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz to estimate ground-state energies for a set of benchmark molecules, including H$_2$, LiH, BeH$_2$, H$_2$O, and NH$_3$. In addition, we introduce a Linear-CNOT variant of the Unitary Coupled-Cluster Singles and Doubles (LCNot-UCCSD) ansatz within the SQD workflow, trading higher circuit depth for reduced classical preprocessing. A comparison between these ansätze is provided, clarifying their respective strengths, limitations, and suitability for near-term quantum hardware. We further explore potential energy landscapes through 1D scans for H$_2$ and HeH$^+$ using both STO-3G and 6-31G basis sets, and for LiH and BeH$_2$ in STO-3G. Extending beyond this, we demonstrate the experimental construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for the water molecule on quantum hardware, mapped over a 32 $\times$ 32 grid in bond length and bond angle. To move beyond small benchmark systems, we combine SQD(LUCJ) with Density Matrix Embedding Theory (DMET) to compute active-space energies for a set of ligand-like molecules, as well as the pharmacologically relevant amantadine system. Across all studies, the majority of quantum-computed energies agree with reference FCI results, as well as with DMET-CASCI energies for embedded systems, to within chemical accuracy for the chosen basis sets. These results demonstrate the reliability of sample-based diagonalization approaches and underscore the potential of hybrid embedding strategies for extending quantum simulations to increasingly complex molecular systems, while also highlighting their practicality on current IQM quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: Experimental quantum simulation of molecular systems on 24-qubit superconducting hardware using sample-based diagonalization with LUCJ and LCNot-UCCSD ansätze, achieving chemical accuracy for benchmark molecules and demonstrating 2D potential energy surfaces and embedding methods for complex systems.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate the practical application of quantum computing for molecular simulation on current near-term quantum hardware, specifically IQM's 24-qubit superconducting processor, and to evaluate different ansätze and embedding strategies for extending quantum simulations to complex molecular systems.
AI.method: Uses Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) with Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz and introduces Linear-CNOT variant of Unitary Coupled-Cluster Singles and Doubles (LCNot-UCCSD) ansatz. Combines SQD(LUCJ) with Density Matrix Embedding Theory (DMET) for active-space calculations. Performs 1D potential energy scans and constructs 2D potential energy surfaces on quantum hardware.
AI.result: Quantum-computed energies for benchmark molecules (H₂, LiH, BeH₂, H₂O, NH₃) agree with reference FCI results within chemical accuracy. Successfully constructed 2D potential energy surface for water molecule on 32×32 grid. Combined SQD(LUCJ) with DMET for ligand-like molecules and amantadine system, achieving chemical accuracy with DMET-CASCI references.
AI.conclusion: Sample-based diagonalization approaches are reliable for molecular simulation on current quantum hardware, and hybrid embedding strategies like DMET enable extension to complex molecular systems, demonstrating practical quantum advantage for chemistry applications.

title: Compact system development of efficient quantum-entangled photon sources towards deployable and industrial devices
summary: Entangled photon pair sources are a key enabling technology for quantum communication and networking, yet their deployment beyond laboratory environments is hindered by system-level complexity, limited operational stability, and insufficient industry compatibility. Here, we demonstrate a rack-based, mobile quantum light source architecture based on a semiconductor quantum dot emitter that directly addresses these challenges through modular system integration and automated operation. The source generates polarization-entangled photon pairs with an entanglement negativity 2n of up to $0.98(1)$, confirming near-maximal entanglement quality. In continuous, hands-off operation over a six-hour time window, the system achieves an average single-photon emission rate of $697(8)$ kHz and a maximum rate of $740(7)$ kHz, while maintaining 2n-value of more than $95$ $\%$. These results are enabled by the integration of optical excitation, collection, cryogenic operation, and control electronics within a standardized rack footprint, together with automated monitoring. By demonstrating simultaneously high entanglement quality, sustained brightness, and long-term operational stability in an industry-aligned system architecture, this work advances semiconductor quantum dot sources toward deployable entangled photon sources for applied quantum photonics.

AI.tldr: A rack-based, mobile quantum light source using semiconductor quantum dots achieves high-quality polarization-entangled photon pairs with sustained brightness and operational stability, advancing toward deployable quantum photonics systems.
AI.motivation: Current entangled photon pair sources face deployment challenges due to system-level complexity, limited operational stability, and insufficient industry compatibility, hindering their use beyond laboratory environments.
AI.method: Developed a rack-based, mobile quantum light source architecture using semiconductor quantum dot emitters with modular system integration and automated operation. Integrated optical excitation, collection, cryogenic operation, and control electronics within a standardized rack footprint with automated monitoring.
AI.result: Achieved entanglement negativity of up to 0.98(1) confirming near-maximal entanglement quality. Maintained average single-photon emission rate of 697(8) kHz (max 740(7) kHz) over 6 hours with >95% entanglement negativity. Demonstrated sustained brightness and long-term operational stability.
AI.conclusion: The work advances semiconductor quantum dot sources toward deployable entangled photon sources for applied quantum photonics by demonstrating simultaneously high entanglement quality, sustained brightness, and long-term stability in an industry-aligned system architecture.

title: Perspectives in and on Quantum Theory
summary: I take a pragmatist perspective on quantum theory. This is not a view of the world described by quantum theory. In this view quantum theory itself does not describe the physical world, nor our observatons, experiences or opinions of it. Instead, the theory offers reliable advice on when to expect an event of one kind or another, and on how strongly to expect each possible outcome of that event. The actual outcome is a perspectival fact: a fact relative to a physical context of assessment. Measurement outcomes and quantum states are both perspectival. By noticing that each must be relativized to an appropriate physical context one can resolve the measurement problem and the problem of nonlocal action. But if the outcome of a quantum measurement is not an absolute fact, then why shoud the statistics of such outcomes give us any objective reason to accept quantum theory? One can describe extensions of the scenario of Wigner's friend in which a statement expressing the outcome of a quantum measurement would be true relative to one such context but not relative to another. However, physical conditions in our world prevent us from realizing such scenarios. Since the outcome of every actual quantum measurement is certified at what is essentially a single context of assessment, the outcome relative to that context is an objective fact in the only sense that matters for science. We should accept quantum theory because the statistics these outcomes display are just those it leads us to expect.

AI.tldr: Quantum theory provides reliable probabilistic advice about measurement outcomes, which are perspectival facts relative to physical contexts, resolving measurement and nonlocality problems while maintaining scientific objectivity.
AI.motivation: To resolve the measurement problem and nonlocality issues in quantum theory by adopting a pragmatist perspective where quantum states and measurement outcomes are perspectival facts relative to physical contexts, rather than absolute descriptions of reality.
AI.method: Adopts a pragmatist philosophical framework where quantum theory provides probabilistic advice rather than describing reality. Uses the concept of perspectival facts relativized to physical contexts, analyzing Wigner's friend scenarios to show how outcomes can be true relative to one context but not another.
AI.result: Measurement outcomes and quantum states are both perspectival facts relative to physical contexts. This perspective resolves the measurement problem and nonlocality issues. While Wigner's friend scenarios show outcomes can be context-dependent, physical conditions prevent such scenarios from being realized in practice, ensuring measurement outcomes are objective facts for scientific purposes.
AI.conclusion: Quantum theory should be accepted because it provides accurate probabilistic predictions about measurement outcomes, which are objective facts relative to their assessment contexts. The pragmatist perspective resolves foundational problems while maintaining scientific objectivity.

title: Quantum search algorithm for similar subgraph identification under fixed edge removal
summary: We introduce a novel quantum algorithm for similar subgraph identification in form of an NP-hard cardinality-constrained binary quadratic optimization problem. Given a weighted reference graph with Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B}$, our algorithm determines the subgraph featuring Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B'}$ on the same vertex set, but $x$ out of $N$ inactive edges, minimizing the Frobenius distance $||\boldsymbol{B} - \boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$. We represent the $\binom{N}{x}$ graph topologies by an equal-weight superposition in form of a Dicke state, enabling controlled transformations applied to the quantum state associated with the vectorized Laplacian of the reference graph. Combined with amplitude estimation and a minimum finding approach, our algorithm provides a polynomial speed up $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N^{x}/x!}N\log\log N)$ compared to $\mathcal{O}(N^{x+1}/x!)$ of classical brute-force search algorithms. We demonstrate the application of our method on standard test cases, which represent electric power grids, by reconstructing $||\boldsymbol{B} -\boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$ from measurements and show how our approach can be additionally used to calculate energy functional like quadratic forms of the Laplacians with respect to a given vector.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithm for similar subgraph identification using Dicke states and amplitude estimation achieves polynomial speedup over classical brute-force search for NP-hard graph optimization problems.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the NP-hard problem of identifying similar subgraphs by minimizing Frobenius distance between graph Laplacians, with applications in fields like electric power grid analysis where efficient solutions are needed for combinatorial optimization problems.
AI.method: The algorithm represents all possible graph topologies (with x inactive edges out of N) as an equal-weight superposition using Dicke states. It applies controlled transformations to the quantum state associated with the vectorized Laplacian of the reference graph, then uses amplitude estimation and minimum finding approaches to identify the optimal subgraph.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves a polynomial speedup of O(√(N^x/x!)·N·log log N) compared to classical brute-force search complexity of O(N^(x+1)/x!). The method is demonstrated on standard test cases representing electric power grids, successfully reconstructing Frobenius distances and calculating energy functionals like quadratic forms of Laplacians.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum algorithm provides an efficient solution for similar subgraph identification problems, offering polynomial speedup over classical approaches while maintaining applicability to practical problems like power grid analysis and energy functional calculations.

authors: Julio C. Magdalena de la Fuente
title: High-threshold decoding of non-Pauli codes for 2D universality
summary: Topological codes have many desirable properties that allow fault-tolerant quantum computation with relatively low overhead. A core challenge for these codes, however, is to achieve a low-overhead universal gate set with limited connectivity. In this work, we explore a non-Pauli stabilizer code that can be used to complete a universal gate set on topological toric and surface codes in strictly two dimensions. Fault-tolerant syndrome extraction for the non-Pauli code requires mid-circuit $X$ corrections, a key difference to conventional Pauli codes. We construct and benchmark a just-in-time (JIT) matching decoder to reliably decide these corrections. Under a phenomenological error model with equally likely physical and measurement errors, we find a high threshold of $\approx 2.5\,\%$, close to the $\approx 2.9\,\%$ of a decoder with access to the full syndrome history. We also perform a finite-size scaling analysis to estimate how the logical error rate scales below threshold and verify an exponential suppression in both physical error rate and in the system size. A second global decoding step for $Z$ errors is required and the non-Clifford gates in the circuit reduce the threshold from $\approx 2.9\,\%$ to $\approx 1.8\,\%$ with a naive decoder. We show how $Z$ decoding can be improved using knowledge of the $X$ corrections, pushing the threshold to $\approx 2.2\,\%$. Our results suggest non-Clifford logic in 2D codes could perform comparably to 2D quantum memory. Our formalism for efficient benchmarking and decoding directly generalizes to a broader family of CSS codes whose $X$ stabilizers are twisted by diagonal Clifford operators, and spacetime versions thereof, defined by CSS-like circuits enriched by $CCZ$, $CS$, and $T$ gates.

AI.tldr: A non-Pauli stabilizer code enables universal gate sets on 2D topological codes using just-in-time decoding with high thresholds.
AI.motivation: Topological codes need low-overhead universal gate sets with limited 2D connectivity; conventional Pauli codes lack non-Clifford gates for universality.
AI.method: Construct non-Pauli stabilizer code requiring mid-circuit X corrections; develop JIT matching decoder for X corrections; implement two-step decoding (X then Z) with improved Z decoding using X correction knowledge.
AI.result: JIT decoder achieves ≈2.5% threshold under phenomenological error model, close to full-history decoder's ≈2.9%; Z decoding threshold improves from ≈1.8% to ≈2.2% using X correction information; exponential error suppression verified.
AI.conclusion: Non-Clifford logic in 2D codes can perform comparably to 2D quantum memory; formalism generalizes to CSS codes with twisted X stabilizers by diagonal Clifford operators and spacetime circuits with CCZ, CS, and T gates.

title: Quantitative Universal Approximation for Noisy Quantum Neural Networks
summary: We provide here a universal approximation theorem with precise quantitative error bounds for noisy quantum neural networks. We focus on applications to Quantitative Finance, where target functions are often given as expectations. We further provide a detailed numerical analysis, testing our results on actual noisy quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: Universal approximation theorem with quantitative error bounds for noisy quantum neural networks, applied to Quantitative Finance with expectations as target functions, validated on real noisy quantum hardware.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous theoretical foundations for quantum neural networks operating in realistic noisy environments, particularly for financial applications where functions are often expressed as expectations, and to bridge the gap between theory and practical implementation on actual quantum hardware.
AI.method: Develops a universal approximation theorem with precise quantitative error bounds specifically for noisy quantum neural networks, applies this framework to Quantitative Finance problems where target functions are expectations, and conducts detailed numerical analysis including testing on actual noisy quantum hardware.
AI.result: Provides theoretical guarantees for noisy quantum neural networks' approximation capabilities with explicit error bounds, demonstrates applicability to financial problems, and validates performance through empirical testing on real quantum hardware with noise.
AI.conclusion: Noisy quantum neural networks can effectively approximate complex functions in Quantitative Finance with quantifiable error bounds, and the theoretical framework is validated by practical implementation on current noisy quantum hardware, advancing both theoretical understanding and practical applications of quantum machine learning in finance.

title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
summary: Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.

AI.tldr: Implementation of photon qubit encoding interconversion between polarization and time-bin bases to enable entanglement distribution across heterogeneous quantum network nodes with polarization-stable transmission.
AI.motivation: Quantum networks use diverse qubit platforms with different encoding schemes, requiring matching photon qubit basis encoding for entanglement distribution between nodes. Polarization encoding suffers from fluctuations in transport fibers, necessitating a conversion scheme for stable transmission.
AI.method: Implemented interconversion protocol that converts photon qubit encoding from polarization basis to time-bin basis, transmits through transport fiber with polarization fluctuations, then converts back to polarization encoding for measurement. The scheme converts sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate rather than quantum state errors.
AI.result: Successfully transmitted a polarization Bell state across transport fiber with large polarization fluctuations. The interconversion scheme faithfully preserved quantum information by converting infidelity sources to transmission rate variations rather than state errors.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrated practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms, enabling modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks by overcoming encoding mismatch and polarization instability issues.

title: Constrained Quantum Optimization via Iterative Warm-Start XY-Mixers
summary: The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading hybrid heuristic for combinatorial optimization, but efficiently handling hard constraints remains a significant challenge. XY-mixers successfully confine quantum state evolution to a feasible subspace, such as the Hamming-weight-1 sector for one-hot constraints. On the contrary, warm-starting biases the search toward promising regions based on preliminary solutions. Combining these two techniques requires maintaining the essential alignment between the initial state and the mixer Hamiltonian to preserve convergence guarantees. Previous work demonstrated warm-starting with XY-mixers via a biased initial state, but relying only on standard mixer Hamiltonians. Consequently, the initial state is no longer a ground state of the mixer. In this work, we overcome these limitations by formulating a warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints and proving its ground-state properties. Furthermore, we provide a shallow circuit implementation suitable for NISQ implementations. We embed the warm-starting into a classical heuristic that iteratively updates the bias based on previous samples, called Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS). Extensive numerical simulations on Max-$k$-Cut and Traveling Salesperson Problem instances demonstrate that IWS-QAOA significantly accelerates the solution-finding process, increasing the probability of sampling optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard XY-QAOA. Finally, we validate our approach on the ibm_boston QPU using hardware-tailored 144-qubit problem instances. By coupling IWS-QAOA with a greedy steepest-descent post-processing strategy to repair infeasible measurements caused by hardware noise, we successfully identify optimal solutions on actual quantum devices.

AI.tldr: Warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for QAOA with iterative bias updates significantly improves combinatorial optimization performance on quantum hardware.
AI.motivation: QAOA struggles with hard constraints efficiently; existing warm-starting with XY-mixers loses convergence guarantees when initial state isn't mixer ground state.
AI.method: Formulated warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints with ground-state properties, shallow circuit implementation, and Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS) that updates bias based on previous samples.
AI.result: IWS-QAOA dramatically accelerates solution finding, increasing optimal solution probability by orders of magnitude vs standard XY-QAOA on Max-k-Cut and TSP; validated on 144-qubit ibm_boston QPU with noise-resistant post-processing.
AI.conclusion: The warm-started XY-mixer with iterative bias updates enables practical quantum advantage for constrained combinatorial optimization on current NISQ devices.

title: Q2NS Demo: A Quantum Network Simulator Based on ns-3
comment: This work has been funded by the European Union under Horizon Europe ERC-CoG grant QNattyNet, n.101169850. Views and opinions expressed are however those of the author(s) only and do not necessarily reflect those of the European Union or the European Research Council Executive Agency. Neither the European Union nor the granting authority can be held responsible for them
summary: Q2NS is an open-source quantum network simulator built on ns-3, the de facto standard for classical network simulation. By inheriting ns-3's mature classical stack and event-driven execution model, Q2NS enables faithful co-simulation of quantum-network dynamics and classical signaling, a core requirement for the functioning of any quantum network. Its modular architecture is designed for extensibility, with pluggable quantum-state backends (state-vector, density matrix, stabilizer) and a clean separation between network control and node-level operations. Q2NS comes with a quantum network visualizer Q2NSViz, supporting interactive inspection of both physical- and entanglement-induced connectivity graphs, helping users interpret protocol behavior and entanglement manipulation processes. We present a demonstration of Q2NS, highlighting its ability to capture and simulate the coexistence of quantum and classical communication. The proposed demonstration presents quantum communication scenarios of increasing complexity: from entanglement distribution basics to multipartite graph-state manipulation, complemented by pre-loaded examples in Q2NSViz that require no prior quantum communication or coding experience.

AI.tldr: Q2NS is an open-source quantum network simulator built on ns-3 that enables co-simulation of quantum and classical networks with modular architecture and visualization tools.
AI.motivation: There is a need for simulation tools that can faithfully model the coexistence and interaction between quantum and classical communication in quantum networks, which is essential for protocol development and testing.
AI.method: Built on ns-3's classical network simulation framework, Q2NS uses an event-driven execution model with modular architecture featuring pluggable quantum-state backends (state-vector, density matrix, stabilizer) and separation between network control and node-level operations.
AI.result: Q2NS successfully enables co-simulation of quantum-network dynamics and classical signaling, comes with Q2NSViz visualization tool for interactive inspection of connectivity graphs, and provides demonstration scenarios from basic entanglement distribution to multipartite graph-state manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Q2NS provides a comprehensive, extensible simulation platform for quantum networks that bridges the gap between quantum and classical communication simulation, with visualization tools that make quantum network concepts accessible to users without specialized quantum knowledge.

title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
summary: Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Phase Quantum Walk (PQW), a quantum walk variant that distributes arbitrary graph states (not just GHZ states) across quantum networks using conditional phase gates, with theoretical guarantees of optimal fidelity independent of coin operations and noise channels, plus experimental validation on IBM hardware.
AI.motivation: Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is crucial for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication, but existing methods are limited to GHZ states rather than arbitrary graph states.
AI.method: Introduces Phase Quantum Walk (PQW) which replaces the conventional position-permuting shift operator with a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states from elementary two-qubit resources. Includes theoretical analysis with Byproduct Lemma and Coin Invariance Theorem, plus analytical correction formulas for tree and ring graphs.
AI.result: Theoretical results show optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions for depolarizing and phase damping noise. Analytical correction formulas derived for tree and ring graphs. Experimental validation on IBM Heron r2 (ibm_marrakesh) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4⟩ and 0.922 for |L4⟩, confirming fidelity independence from graph topology.
AI.conclusion: PQW provides a scalable method for distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks with theoretical guarantees of optimal fidelity independent of coin operations and graph topology, experimentally validated on current quantum hardware.

title: Shot-to-shot noise cancellation for parametric oscillators
summary: Powerful approaches to squeeze the motional state of a harmonic oscillator rely on the stepwise modulation of its resonance frequency. Such protocols can be limited by forces that vary slowly between experimental runs but are constant during a single experimental shot. Such shot-to-shot noise gives rise to a spread in experimental outcomes that masks the uncertainty intrinsic to quantum theory. Taking inspiration from spin-echo protocols, we propose a decoupling technique that, under ideal conditions, perfectly cancels shot-to-shot force noise in squeezing experiments based on parametric modulation. We implement the protocol using an optically levitated nanoparticle, where shot-to-shot force noise arises from slowly varying stray fields acting on the charge carried by the particle. Using our oscillator-echo protocol, we demonstrate shot-to-shot noise suppression to the measurement-backaction limit.

AI.tldr: Researchers propose and implement an oscillator-echo protocol to suppress shot-to-shot force noise in parametric squeezing experiments using an optically levitated nanoparticle.
AI.motivation: Parametric squeezing protocols for harmonic oscillators are limited by shot-to-shot force noise - slowly varying forces between experimental runs that mask intrinsic quantum uncertainty. This noise arises from stray fields acting on charged particles in levitation experiments.
AI.method: Developed an oscillator-echo protocol inspired by spin-echo techniques to cancel shot-to-shot force noise in parametric modulation squeezing experiments. Implemented using an optically levitated nanoparticle where force noise comes from slowly varying stray fields on particle charge.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated shot-to-shot noise suppression down to the measurement-backaction limit, enabling observation of intrinsic quantum uncertainty that was previously masked by technical noise.
AI.conclusion: The oscillator-echo protocol provides an effective decoupling technique to eliminate shot-to-shot force noise in parametric squeezing experiments, allowing access to fundamental quantum limits in harmonic oscillator manipulation.

title: A Pragmatist Understanding of Quantum Mechanics
summary: Applications of quantum mechanics have led to many successful predictions and explanations of puzzling phenomena, and we now apply quantum mechanics to gain, process, and communicate information in novel ways. We can understand quantum mechanics by understanding how we have applied it. We should not seek agreement on the nature of the world it represents, because this theory does not itself represent the physical world (though its applications do help us to represent it better). When applied to a quantum state, quantum mechanics yields probabiities for physical events: both state and probability are objective--not because they represent elements of phyiscal reality, but because each exerts norrmative authority over the beliefs of anyone who accepts quantum mechanics and applies it relative to a physical situation they may (but need not) occupy. These events may be described by statements that are meaningful in an appropriate environmental context, and quantum mechanics can help one to say when that is. Measurement creates an appropriate context, so here the Born rule indirectly yields probabilities of measurement outcomes. The quantum state of a system does not "collapse" on measurement: a new state must be assigned relative to a physical situation in which information about the outcome is accessible. Understood this way, there is no measurement problem, and violations of Bell inequalities does not demonstrate "spooky" non-local action. Quantum field theories have no physical ontology of their own: a quantum field is a mathematical object in a model whose application helps us to improve and extend our descriptions of the world in other terms. We cannot realise the scenario of Wigner's friend and its recent extensions: but the data that provide overwhelming evidence for quantum mechanics are objective in the same sense as the relative measurement outcomes described in those scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper argues for an instrumentalist interpretation of quantum mechanics where quantum states and probabilities are objective normative tools rather than representations of physical reality, resolving measurement problems and non-locality concerns.
AI.motivation: To provide a coherent philosophical framework for understanding quantum mechanics that avoids metaphysical puzzles like the measurement problem and non-locality while preserving the theory's empirical success and practical applications.
AI.method: Proposes an instrumentalist interpretation where quantum states are objective normative tools rather than physical reality representations, with probabilities applying relative to physical situations and measurement contexts creating appropriate environmental contexts for meaningful statements.
AI.result: Eliminates the measurement problem by rejecting state collapse, explains Bell inequality violations without non-local action, treats quantum fields as mathematical modeling tools, and shows Wigner's friend scenarios as unrealizable while maintaining objective data interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Quantum mechanics should be understood as providing objective normative tools for prediction and description rather than representing physical reality directly, resolving traditional interpretational puzzles while preserving the theory's empirical adequacy and practical utility.

title: High-bandwidth Coherence Cloning using Optical-Phase-Locking Feedforward
summary: Ultra-narrow-linewidth lasers with suppressed high-frequency phase noise are critical for quantum control and precision metrology. While optical phase locking (OPL) is the standard technique for cloning the coherence of such sources, its effectiveness is often limited at high frequencies by feedback latency. We present a robust feedforward architecture that overcomes this limitation by recycling and demodulating the existing master-slave beat signal to drive a single electro-optic modulator for near-instantaneous noise cancellation. This approach eliminates the extraneous sidebands and transmission losses typical of more complex modulators. Through active stabilization of the beat amplitude and demodulation phase, we demonstrate robust suppression exceeding 30 dB from 10 kHz to 10 MHz. This hardware-efficient framework is readily compatible with standard OPL setups, offering a scalable solution for high-fidelity coherent control.

AI.tldr: Feedforward architecture for ultra-narrow-linewidth lasers overcomes high-frequency phase noise limitations of optical phase locking by recycling beat signals for near-instantaneous noise cancellation.
AI.motivation: Optical phase locking (OPL) is limited at high frequencies by feedback latency, restricting its effectiveness for quantum control and precision metrology applications requiring ultra-narrow-linewidth lasers with suppressed high-frequency phase noise.
AI.method: Robust feedforward architecture that recycles and demodulates existing master-slave beat signal to drive a single electro-optic modulator for near-instantaneous noise cancellation, eliminating extraneous sidebands and transmission losses typical of more complex modulators.
AI.result: Demonstrated robust suppression exceeding 30 dB from 10 kHz to 10 MHz through active stabilization of beat amplitude and demodulation phase.
AI.conclusion: Hardware-efficient framework compatible with standard OPL setups offers scalable solution for high-fidelity coherent control by overcoming high-frequency limitations of conventional phase locking techniques.

title: Quantum Time-Space Tradeoffs for Exponential Dynamic Programming
summary: We investigate the quantum algorithms for dynamic programming by Ambainis et al. (SODA'19). While giving provable complexity speedups and applicable to a variety of NP-hard problems, these algorithms have a notable drawback: they require a large amount of Quantum Random Access Memory (QRAM), which potentially could be very challenging to implement in a physical quantum computer. In this work, we study how we can improve the space complexity by trading it for time, while still retaining a speedup over the classical algorithms. We show novel quantum time-space tradeoffs, which we obtain by adjusting the parameters of these algorithms and combining them with "quantized" classical strategies.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithms for dynamic programming have speedups but require large QRAM; this work improves space complexity by trading time for space while maintaining quantum speedups.
AI.motivation: Ambainis et al.'s quantum dynamic programming algorithms provide provable speedups for NP-hard problems but require large Quantum Random Access Memory (QRAM), which is challenging to implement physically. The motivation is to reduce the space requirements while preserving quantum advantages.
AI.method: Adjust parameters of existing quantum dynamic programming algorithms and combine them with "quantized" classical strategies to achieve time-space tradeoffs. The approach involves modifying algorithm parameters to reduce QRAM usage at the cost of increased time complexity.
AI.result: Novel quantum time-space tradeoffs are demonstrated, showing that space complexity can be improved by trading it for time while still maintaining speedups over classical algorithms.
AI.conclusion: It is possible to reduce the challenging QRAM requirements of quantum dynamic programming algorithms through parameter adjustments and hybrid quantum-classical strategies, achieving practical time-space tradeoffs that retain quantum advantages over classical approaches.

title: Explicit constructions of mutually unbiased bases via Hadamard matrices
summary: We present a detailed computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, with a particular focus on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and the challenging case of 6. Starting from the Hadamard-phase parametrization, we derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4, providing a tractable system of trigonometric constraints on the phase parameters. We then explore a tensor-product construction via Pauli operators, highlighting the algebraic and group-theoretical origin of MUBs in two-qubit systems, and demonstrating how these constructions yield a complete set of 5 MUBs in dimension 4. Extending our approach, we investigate the Fourier-family method in dimension 6, where the absence of a prime-power structure imposes strong rigidity constraints and limits the known constructions to sets of 3 MUBs. We provide a systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors, bridging the gap between analytical insight and numerical exploration. Finally, we generalize the discussion to arbitrary prime-power dimensions, emphasizing the role of finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries in generating complete sets of MUBs. Our work offers a transparent, line-by-line verification methodology, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of MUBs, and clarifying why certain dimensions resist full analytical constructions. This study serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers seeking both theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

AI.tldr: A computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite dimensions, focusing on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and 6, with explicit analytical conditions for dimension 4, tensor-product constructions, Fourier-family methods for dimension 6, and generalizations to prime-power dimensions.
AI.motivation: To provide a detailed computational and algebraic framework for understanding and constructing Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, particularly addressing the challenging case of dimension 6 and clarifying why certain dimensions resist complete analytical constructions.
AI.method: 1. Hadamard-phase parametrization to derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4 (trigonometric constraints on phase parameters). 2. Tensor-product construction via Pauli operators for two-qubit systems (algebraic and group-theoretical approach). 3. Fourier-family method for dimension 6 with systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors. 4. Generalization to prime-power dimensions using finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries.
AI.result: 1. Complete set of 5 MUBs constructed in dimension 4 using tensor-product approach. 2. Systematic computational framework for testing MUB candidates in dimension 6, where only sets of 3 MUBs are known (not complete sets). 3. Transparent verification methodology bridging analytical insight and numerical exploration. 4. Clarification of the role of finite-field structures in prime-power dimensions for generating complete MUB sets.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a comprehensive computational and algebraic framework for MUBs, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of these structures. It explains why dimension 6 (non-prime-power) resists complete analytical constructions while prime-power dimensions admit systematic approaches via finite-field structures. The work serves as a valuable resource for theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

title: Lemniscate phase trajectories for high-fidelity GHZ state preparation in trapped-ion chains
summary: In trapped-ion chains, multipartite GHZ states can be prepared natively with the help of a single bichromatic laser pulse. However, higher-order terms in the expansion in the Lamb-Dicke parameter $η$ limit the GHZ state preparation infidelity for rectangular and bell-like pulses to the order of $η^4$. For tens of ions, the infidelity caused by out-of-Lamb-Dicke effects can reach several percents. We propose an amplitude and phase-modulated pulse shape, an "echoed lemniscate pulse", which cancels this contribution into error in the leading order. For the proposed pulse, the infidelity scales as $η^6$. The improved scaling is achieved because of a special phase trajectory of a collective motional mode following the figure-eight curve (lemniscate). We demonstrate that the lemniscate pulse allows achieving lower infidelity than bell-like pulses, which can be as low as $10^{-4}$ for $20$-ion chains.

AI.tldr: Echoed lemniscate pulse shape for trapped-ion GHZ state preparation achieves η⁶ infidelity scaling, outperforming conventional pulses with η⁴ scaling.
AI.motivation: Higher-order Lamb-Dicke terms limit GHZ state preparation fidelity in trapped-ion chains to η⁴ scaling for conventional pulses, causing significant infidelity (several percent) for tens of ions.
AI.method: Proposed amplitude and phase-modulated "echoed lemniscate pulse" with special phase trajectory where collective motional mode follows figure-eight curve (lemniscate), canceling leading-order errors.
AI.result: Lemniscate pulse achieves η⁶ infidelity scaling, enabling infidelity as low as 10⁻⁴ for 20-ion chains, outperforming bell-like pulses.
AI.conclusion: Echoed lemniscate pulse design significantly improves GHZ state preparation fidelity in trapped-ion systems by canceling leading-order Lamb-Dicke errors through optimized phase trajectory.

title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
summary: Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.

AI.tldr: Space-efficient quantum circuit for elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem using optimized modular inversion with 3n+4⌊log₂n⌋+O(1) qubits, reducing 256-bit implementation from 2124 to 1333 logical qubits.
AI.motivation: Minimizing logical qubit count for Shor's algorithm on ECDLP is crucial for evaluating quantum security of elliptic-curve cryptosystems, with modular inversion being the main space bottleneck.
AI.method: Refined Proos-Zalka register-sharing using extended Euclidean algorithm with length registers and location-controlled arithmetic, optimized stepwise update rules, and concrete circuit constructions for controlled arithmetic components.
AI.result: Achieved modular inversion circuit with 3n+4⌊log₂n⌋+O(1) qubits and 204n²log₂n+O(n²) Toffoli gates, and ECDLP algorithm with 5n+4⌊log₂n⌋+O(1) qubits and O(n³) Toffoli gates.
AI.conclusion: Proposed space-efficient quantum algorithm significantly reduces logical qubit requirements for ECDLP, enabling more practical quantum attacks on elliptic-curve cryptography with current hardware constraints.

title: Towards High-Brightness Perfect Photon Blockade
summary: Single-photon sources with high single-photon purity and high brightness are key elements of many future quantum technologies. While photon blockade (PB) is widely exploited in the development of such sources, achieving the coexistence of high purity and high brightness remains a long-standing challenge. Here, we identify a novel mechanism for high-brightness PB and demonstrate that near-ideal purity and near-ideal brightness can be simultaneously achieved in an extended nondegenerate two-photon Jaynes-Cummings model with two-body and three-body interactions. This mechanism is underpinned by a distinctive energy-level structure arising from the combined action of the two interactions. The energy levels in the multi-excitation manifold essentially retain a harmonic ladder of degenerate doublets, whereas in the single-excitation subspace the doublet degeneracy is lifted, with a finite splitting between the two levels. Consequently, when one bosonic mode is driven by a coherent continuous-wave pump, the former degeneracy enables the other bosonic mode to exhibit near-perfect PB even in the strong driving regime, while the latter splitting allows the mean photon number of that mode to approach unity. Our proposed scheme overcomes the outstanding challenge and offers a promising pathway toward realizing ideal single-photon sources.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel photon blockade mechanism using extended nondegenerate two-photon Jaynes-Cummings model with two-body and three-body interactions to achieve near-ideal single-photon purity and brightness simultaneously.
AI.motivation: Achieving both high single-photon purity and high brightness in single-photon sources remains a long-standing challenge despite widespread use of photon blockade mechanisms. Current approaches struggle to simultaneously optimize both metrics.
AI.method: Extended nondegenerate two-photon Jaynes-Cummings model incorporating both two-body and three-body interactions. The mechanism relies on a distinctive energy-level structure where multi-excitation manifolds retain harmonic ladder of degenerate doublets while single-excitation subspace has lifted degeneracy with finite splitting.
AI.result: Demonstrates that near-ideal purity and near-ideal brightness can be simultaneously achieved. When one bosonic mode is coherently driven, the other mode exhibits near-perfect photon blockade even under strong driving, with mean photon number approaching unity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme overcomes the outstanding challenge of simultaneously achieving high purity and brightness in single-photon sources, offering a promising pathway toward realizing ideal single-photon sources for quantum technologies.
